title: Bonding two rabbits
subtitle: Introducing rabbits to each other without fighting.
excerpt: Two rabbits can live together happily once bonded, but an introduction without preparation results in a damaging fight within seconds. The bonding process uses a neutral space, stress bonding techniques, and very gradual increases in shared time. A successful bond takes anywhere from a few days to several weeks depending on the rabbits.
sourceNotes: RSPCA rabbit welfare guidance, rspca.org.uk. Rabbit Welfare Association and Fund (RWAF), rwaf.org.uk, bonding guide. DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Rabbits (2019).

glossaryTerms.definition: A space that neither rabbit has previously occupied or scent-marked. Bonding in a rabbit's own run or hutch triggers territorial aggression; a bathroom, a new pen, or a car boot are commonly used neutral spaces.

content.text: Rabbits are territorial and will fight any unfamiliar rabbit without introduction. A bonded pair, once established, provides companionship, reduces stress-related behaviours, and is actively recommended by the RSPCA and RWAF over solo housing. The process requires patience and cannot be rushed.

Both rabbits should be neutered before bonding. Neutering (spaying for females, castration for males) eliminates hormonal aggression and reduces territorial behaviour. Wait 6 to 8 weeks after neutering before attempting introduction. Neuter first, bond second.

for short supervised sessions of 10 to 15 minutes. Some circling, chasing, and mounting is normal assertive behaviour that establishes the hierarchy; a clear dominant and submissive rabbit settling into their roles is progress. Stop and separate if you see sustained mounting with accompanying fur-pulling, circling with ears flat and teeth chattering, or any biting. Use a board or a second person to separate if needed, do not reach into a fight with bare hands.

Many bonding guides suggest placing both rabbits in a carrier in a moving car for 15 to 20 minutes. The mild shared stress of the journey occupies their attention and has a well-documented calming effect on inter-rabbit aggression. After the car ride, return to the neutral space; pairs often groom each other for the first time within the hour following this method.

Increase session length gradually over days. When both rabbits groom each other (the final sign of a real bond), move them into a cleaned and rearranged version of one rabbit's housing. Rearranging removes scent marks and makes the space feel neutral. Watch overnight access for the first week before leaving them alone.

Some pairs will not bond. If 6 weeks of structured introduction produce no progress and fights remain serious at every session, the pairing may not work. Rabbits with a history of fighting other rabbits, unneutered males, and some individual temperaments may never cohabit safely. Side-by-side housing in adjoining pens with a mesh divider allows social contact without risk; some rabbits appear content with this arrangement.
